days.passage: Investigators found that a series of mistakes led to the sinking of the Titanic. A wireless message had come in from a French liner, warning of ice ahead, but that was a thousand miles away, and so, no need to worry. On April 13, the vessel Rapphannock also warned the Titanic of dangerous ice ahead. On the following day, there came a spate* of other warnings from a Cunard ship, a Dutch liner, and the White Star Baltic—all telling of icebergs about 250 miles from the Titanic’s current position. Next came the German Amerika, echoing the same forebodings, followed by the California, cautioning the Titanic about the field ice. Finally, the Mesaba called attention to an enormous belt of ice stretching directly across the Titanic’s path. All the messages emanating from sister ships should have had a profound* effect on Smith and company. No one miscreant could be fingered, but a host of crew members were certainly blameworthy. Why didn’t Captain Smith’s officers react to those messages? Notations were indeed made on slips of paper but largely ignored and forgotten. There was no standard protocol for the handling of such messages; if there had been, Captain Smith would certainly have taken a circuitous route so as to avoid the dangerous icebergs.

days.passageZh: 调查人员发现，一系列错误导致了泰坦尼克号的沉没。一艘法国客轮发出无线电警告前方有冰，但那距离已远千里，无需担心。4月13日，拉帕诺克号也警告泰坦尼克号前方有危险的冰层。第二天，来自一艘坎纳德船、一艘荷兰客轮和白星波罗的海号的一系列警告——都显示距离泰坦尼克号当前位置约250英里处有冰山。接着是德国的美国号，同样令人担忧，接着是加利福尼亚号，提醒泰坦尼克号注意场地冰层。最后，梅萨巴号引起了人们注意，一条巨大的冰带正横贯泰坦尼克号航线。所有来自姊妹舰的信息本应对史密斯和他的同伴产生深远影响。没有一个恶棍可以被指认，但许多船员无疑都应承担责任。为什么史密斯船长的军官们对这些信息没有反应？确实有纸条上的记号，但大多被忽视和遗忘。处理此类消息没有统一的标准协议;如果有，史密斯船长肯定会绕远路避开危险的冰山。

days.passage: And then it happened. White in its innocence, a monstrous iceberg smashed into the luxury liner, ripping an ugly gash of 250 feet along the starboard and causing a fatal wound. Within seconds, thousands of cubic feet of water had penetrated the shattered hull. One after another, dominolike, the watertight compartments and bulkhead were flooded. The unthinkable had happened despite the absolute guarantees of the shipbuilders, Harland & Woolf. There followed a macabre scene as the ship’s band, clad in their tuxedos, continued to play show tunes while hordes of terrified passengers, many in nightclothes, rushed toward the lifeboats. The crew called out, “Women and children first,” but their lack of an orderly plan for loading would have profound* ramifications. In fact, some boats that could hold 30 were sent into the Atlantic with only a handful of people—generally first-class passengers. As panic began to take hold, the realization that there weren’t enough lifeboats exacerbated* the situation, bringing out the worst in a rapacious few. Several insurgent males ignored the crew and jumped into descending lifeboats. It was an act of shame they would have to live with for the rest of their lives.

days.passageZh: 然后，事情发生了。一块纯净无邪的巨大冰山撞击豪华邮轮，撕裂了右舷250英尺的丑陋伤口，造成致命伤。几秒钟内，数千立方英尺的水渗透进破碎的船体。一个接一个，像多米诺骨牌一样，水密舱室和舱壁被淹没。尽管造船厂哈兰德与伍尔夫公司给予绝对保证，但不可思议的事情还是发生了。随后出现了一幕阴森的场景，船上的乐队穿着燕尾服继续演奏音乐剧曲目，而成群结队的惊恐乘客，许多穿着睡衣，冲向救生艇。船员们喊道：“妇女和儿童优先”，但他们没有有序的装载计划，将带来深远的影响。事实上，有些能载30人的船只只载着少数人——通常是头等舱乘客——进入大西洋。随着恐慌蔓延，意识到救生艇数量不足，情况更加恶化，少数贪婪者展现出最坏的一面。几名叛乱男性无视船员，跳上下潜的救生艇。这是他们必须伴随一生的羞耻行为。

days.passage: As one might have expected, manifold* law suits against the White Star Line began to crop up within weeks of the sinking and rescue. The glut of billionaires on board (Astors, Wideners, Guggenheims, Strausses, et al.) did not file any claims, but other cases went all the way to the Supreme Court and kept lawyers and judges busy for the next four years. The average claim had been for a modest $1500, and the average award, paid by the White Star Line, was a risible $1000. White Star’s top notch legal staff was accused of using dilatory tactics, tiring the claimants until they agreed to settle for a mere pittance*. Their lawyers called many claims specious and rejected them out of hand. The denouement of the story is rather sad. American and British maritime law had long given special protection to ship owners on the grounds that their business was such a risky one. And so there was a limit to the amount of money that White Star could be assessed. In the end, they paid only 4% of the $16 million originally demanded by the survivors and were happy to close the books on the ocean disaster. We can imagine that if a similar tragedy were to take place today, the settlements would be in the hundreds of millions.

days.passageZh: 正如人们所预料的那样，在沉没和救援发生数周内，针对白星航运的多起诉讼开始出现。船上众多亿万富翁（阿斯特、怀德纳、古根海姆、施特劳斯等）未提出索赔，但其他案件一路上诉至最高法院，令律师和法官忙碌了四年。平均索赔金额为1500美元，而白星航运公司支付的平均赔偿金额则是荒谬至极的1000美元。白星顶级法律团队被指控采用拖延策略，耗尽原告疲惫，最终同意接受极低的和解*。他们的律师称许多主张站不住脚，并断然驳回。故事的结局相当令人悲伤。美国和英国的海事法长期以来一直给予船东特殊保护，理由是他们的业务风险极大。因此，白星公司可被评估的金额是有限的。最终，他们只支付了幸存者最初要求的1600万美元中的4%，并乐于结束这场海洋灾难的报案。我们可以想象，如果今天发生类似的悲剧，定居点将达到数亿美元。

days.story:
A study ______ from the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene declared that one in eight adults in our major cities has diabetes, a ______ disease. Many, however, are not aware that they have it or how ______ it can be. There is a ______ of evidence revealing that the high blood sugar that affects diabetics is more characteristic of Asian Americans, African-Americans, and Hispanics than of the white population.

Unfortunately, many of the victims of diabetes do not take immediate steps to deal with the disease. New York City’s health commissioner has warned of the ______ of a failure to control the high blood sugar: blindness, amputations, and heart disease. In fact, diabetes is the nation’s fastest growing major disease.
days.storyZh:
卫生与心理卫生部______一项研究指出，我们主要城市中每八个成年人中就有一人患有糖尿病，这是一种______疾病。然而，许多人并不知道自己患有这种病，也不知道它有多______。有______证据表明，影响糖尿病患者的高血糖在亚裔美国人、非裔美国人和西班牙裔中更为典型，而非白人群体。

不幸的是，许多糖尿病患者并未立即采取措施应对该疾病。纽约市卫生专员警告称，失明、截肢和心脏病可能导致______高血糖控制。事实上，糖尿病是该国增长最快的主要疾病。

days.passage: On Palm Sunday, April 9, 1865, General Ulysses S. Grant sent a terse* dispatch to Secretary of War Edwin Stanton. It contained the long-awaited sentence that the Confederate General Robert E. Lee had surrendered. The dolorous Civil War that had crippled the young nation was finally over. President Lincoln was only 56 at the time, but he looked twenty years older. The burden of being a wartime president had so enervated Lincoln that Surgeon General Barnes feared an imminent* nervous breakdown. When Grant’s news reached Lincoln, he went to the front windows of the White House and waved to the crowd below. He proceeded to make a brief speech about the problems of Reconstruction and advocated* the granting of suffrage to Negro soldiers. Among the listeners was a Southern patriot, the popular actor John Wilkes Booth, almost as famous in the theater as his father, Junius. “That’s the last speech he will ever make,” said Booth to a fellow member of his cabal of conspirators. Booth’s odious plan was to assassinate Lincoln whom he hated passionately, while an associate, George Atzerodt, would do the same to Vice-President Andrew Johnson.

days.passageZh: 1865年4月9日棕枝主日，尤利西斯·S·格兰特将军向战争部长埃德温·斯坦顿发出简短的电报。信中包含了期待已久的判决，即邦联将军罗伯特·E·李投降。那场摧毁这个年轻国家的悲惨内战终于结束了。当时林肯总统只有56岁，但看起来比他老二十岁。作为战时总统的重担让林肯疲惫不堪，外科总监巴恩斯担心他即将精神崩溃。当格兰特的消息传到林肯耳中时，他走到白宫前窗，向下面的人群挥手致意。他随后发表了简短演讲，谈及重建时期的问题，并主张*赋予黑人士兵选举权。听众中有一位南方爱国者、著名演员约翰·威尔克斯·布斯，他在戏剧界几乎和他的父亲朱尼厄斯一样出名。“那是他最后一次演讲，”布斯对他阴谋集团的另一位成员说。布斯恶心的计划是暗杀他极度痛恨的林肯，而他的同伙乔治·阿策罗特则会对副总统安德鲁·约翰逊做同样的事。

days.passage: Lincoln’s family and friends remembered that the President had a prescient dream in March, several weeks before the fatal day, and provided them with a verbatim account. He told of entering the East Room in the White House where a throng* of people were gathered around an open coffin. In his reverie, Lincoln asked a soldier, “Who is dead in the White House?” “The President,” was the reply. “He was killed by an assassin.” Mrs. Lincoln said, “I’m glad I don’t believe in dreams or I should be in terror from this time forth.” Lincoln’s was the calming voice, “Let’s try to forget it. I think the Lord in His own good time and way will work this out all right.” Of course, all who loved Abe Lincoln would have been deeply agitated if they had known what John Wilkes Booth was planning. As a Southern secessionist, he despised the President. As a thespian, he romanticized the action that he could take to rid the nation of a cruel warmonger. Although he had not taken an active part in the Civil War, he was convinced that he could contribute to the Confederate cause by kidnapping the bearded despot. It wasn’t exactly clear in his mind whether he would “capture” Lincoln and take him to Richmond where he could be exchanged for Confederate prisoners of war—or whether he would just put a bullet in the President’s head.

days.passageZh: 林肯的家人和朋友记得总统在三月做了一个预见性的梦，也就是致命事件发生前几周，并逐字描述了他们。他讲述了进入白宫东厅时，那里聚集着一大群人围着一口敞开的棺材。林肯在沉思中问一名士兵：“白宫里死了谁？”“总统，”回答。“他被刺客杀害了。”林肯夫人说，“我很庆幸我不相信梦境，否则从现在起我就该感到恐惧了。”林肯的声音安抚着，“我们试着忘了它。我相信主会以祂自己的好时机和方式解决这一切。”当然，所有爱亚伯拉罕·林肯的人如果知道约翰·威尔克斯·布斯的计划，都会非常激动。作为南方的分裂主义者，他痛恨总统。作为一名戏剧演员，他浪漫化了自己能采取的行动来清除残酷的战争贩子。虽然他未积极参与内战，但他坚信通过绑架这位留胡须的暴君可以为邦联事业做出贡献。他并不清楚自己是否会“俘获”林肯并带到里士满，在那里与南方邦联战俘交换，还是直接朝总统头部开枪。

days.passage: The pathological yet articulate Booth had rounded up several co-conspirators and shared his delusions of grandeur with them. He had produced a polemic that convinced his crew that it would be a patriotic thing to capture the President. One of them was assigned to shut off the master gas valve at Ford’s Theatre when Mr. and Mrs Lincoln were seated there at the play. With all the lights out, Booth would bind and gag the President. Two men would lower Lincoln onto the stage, and then carry him out the rear door to a covered wagon waiting in the alley. They would head for Port Tobacco and then ferry across the Potomac to their ultimate* destination, Richmond, Virginia. Several dry runs* had not worked out for the cabalists* who were about to reach an impasse when Booth learned that Lincoln would be celebrating General Grant’s victories with a party at Ford’s Theater on the night of April 14. He promised the small group that destiny was at hand; their bold act, he said, would make their names famous forever in the annals of U.S. history. In the late afternoon of April 14, Booth watched a rehearsal of the play that would be performed that evening. He had reviewed his action plan and the escape route, and he believed it to be foolproof. He mouthed the phrase he would use after killing Lincoln, “Sic Semper Tyrannis” (“Thus always to tyrants”). The curtain was about to go up on one of the darkest days in the country’s history.

days.passageZh: 这位病态却口齿伶俐的布斯召集了几位共谋者，向他们分享了他的自大妄想。他发表了一篇论战文章，说服了他的团队，认为抓捕总统是一种爱国行为。其中一人被指派在福特剧院关闭主煤气阀，当时林肯夫妇正在观看戏剧。灯光全灭时，布斯会绑住并堵住总统的嘴。两名男子将林肯放到舞台上，然后从后门抬到巷子里等候的篷车上。他们将前往烟草港，然后乘渡轮渡过波托马克河，抵达最终目的地——弗吉尼亚州里士满。几次演练*对卡巴拉派*来说都未能奏效*，他们正要陷入僵局时，布斯得知林肯将在4月14日晚于福特剧院举办庆祝格兰特将军胜利的派对。他向小团体承诺命运就在眼前;他说，他们的大胆举动将使他们的名字永远载入美国历史史册。4月14日下午晚些时候，布斯观看了当晚将上演的戏剧排练。他复审了自己的行动计划和逃生路线，认为这是万无一失的。他默念着杀死林肯后会用的短语：“Sic Semper Tyrannis”（“暴君永远如此”）。在该国历史上最黑暗的一天之一，幕布即将拉开。

days.passage: At 8:25 the Lincolns arrived at the theater. When they entered Booths 7 & 8, as regimen dictated, the band played “Hail to the Chief.” The 1675 members of the audience stood to honor the great man, and then the play commenced. It is reported that Booth said to a drunk who had denigrated his acting skill, “When I leave the stage, I will be the most famous man in America.” At about 10 P.M., with extreme guile, Booth had managed to be behind Box 7 in the darkness of the hallway. He saw the silhouette of a head above the horsehair rocker. Derringer in his hand, he aimed it between the President’s left ear and his spine. The shot was drowned out by laughter on the stage. Shouting “Revenge for the South,” Booth climbed over the ledge of the box and jumped onto the stage, breaking his leg in the process. In pain, Booth limped out the stage door where his horse was waiting and made his getaway. Days later, however, he was cornered in a Virginia barn and shot. Three of the cabal* members were arrested and hanged. At the theater, a 23-year-old doctor attended to the wounded President. He found that the lead shot had lodged in Lincoln’s brain, a bad sign. Several soldiers carried Mr. Lincoln across the street to a private house. His family physician came and so did the Surgeon General. The President struggled throughout the long night, but it was apparent that a mortal wound had been inflicted, and he could not be saved. At 7:22 A.M. it was over; two silver coins were placed on the assassinated President’s eyes. Then Secretary Stanton uttered the famous words, “Now he belongs to the ages.”

days.passageZh: 8：25，林肯一家抵达剧院。当他们按照规矩进入第7和第8号录音室时，乐队演奏了《向酋长致敬》。1675名观众起立向这位伟人致敬，随后戏剧开始。据报道，布斯对一位贬低他演技的醉汉说：“当我离开舞台时，我将成为美国最著名的人。”大约晚上10点，布斯极其狡猾地躲到了7号包厢后面的走廊黑暗中。他看到马毛摇滚车上方有一个头的轮廓。他手中握着德林格枪，瞄准总统左耳与脊柱之间。舞台上的笑声盖过了这枪声。布斯大喊“为南方复仇”，翻过包厢的边缘跳上舞台，途中摔断了腿。布斯痛苦地一瘸一拐地从马厩门口逃离，马匹正等着他，随后逃离了。然而几天后，他被困在弗吉尼亚的一个谷仓中并被枪杀。三名阴谋*成员被逮捕并绞死。在剧院，一名23岁的医生为受伤的总统提供治疗。他发现铅弹卡在林肯脑中，这是个坏兆头。几名士兵将林肯先生抬过街道，送到一处私人住宅。他的家庭医生来了，外科医生总医师也来了。总统整夜挣扎，但显然他受了致命伤，无法挽救。早上7：22，比赛结束;两枚银币被放在被暗杀总统的眼睛上。随后斯坦顿部长说出了那句著名的话：“现在他属于时代。”

days.story:
Do you know what a “perk” is? Simply put, it’s an extra reward, a special benefit given to sweeten the job for an employee. Now an ______ staffer at Serus, a software maker in California’s Silicon Valley, has skillfully described an incredible perk given to him and his fellow workers—a thrill-packed parachute plunge as they jumped from a plane 14,000 feet above the ground.

“Our employees work hard and can become ______ ,” said a Serus executive, “and we want to invigorate them with sky dives, as well as cruises, beauty treatments at spas, birthday parties, maid services, and other creative perks that our ______ might conjure up.”

Of course, company executives are deeply interested in keeping productive staff members from quitting and going to work for competitors. And so, the host of perks they offer reflect the ______ behind their generosity. “Cash bonuses won’t have the same effect,” a CEO said. In a ______ remark he declared, “It’s like a parent who throws money at his child when what the youngster really wants is attention.”
days.storyZh:
你知道“特权”是什么吗？简单来说，这是一种额外的奖励，一种特别的福利，用来让员工更享受工作。现在，加州硅谷软件制造商Serus的一名______员工，他巧妙地描述了自己和同事们获得的一项惊人福利——从离地面14,000英尺高空的飞机上跳伞，体验惊险刺激的降落伞。

“我们的员工工作努力，可以成为______，”一位Serus高管说，“我们希望通过跳伞、游轮、水疗美容、生日派对、保姆服务以及______可能带来的其他创意福利来激励他们。”

当然，公司高管非常关心让高效员工不辞职去竞争对手工作。因此，他们提供的众多福利反映了他们慷慨背后的背后______。一位CEO表示：“现金奖金不会有同样的效果。”他在______的评论中说：“这就像一个父母给孩子扔钱，而孩子真正想要的是关注。”

replenish.definition: To refill; to renew; to supply again or to add a fresh quantity to.

stunted.definition: checked in natural growth, held back in growth

mercenary.definition: One motivated by gain, especially monetary.

libel.definition: A written or pictorial false statement which unjustly seeks to damage someone's reputation.

dulcet.definition: sweet or melodious to the ear

rail.definition: A horizontal bar extending between supports and used for support or as a barrier; a railing.

derived.definition: descended from, received from a source

internecine.definition: involving conflict within a group, mutually destructive

encomiums.definition: Warm praise, especially a formal expression of such praise; a tribute.

genocide.definition: planned destruction of an entire people

forebodings.definition: A sense of evil to come.
